title: The Bible
description: A foundational scriptural collection in Judaism and Christianity, translated and contested across centuries.
descriptionParagraphs: The Bible is not a single book in the modern sense but a many-layered scriptural library made up of law codes, poems, prophecies, wisdom literature, gospels, letters, and apocalyptic visions. Across those genres it gathers stories of creation, covenant, kingship, exile, judgment, mercy, redemption, and the struggle to live under divine command in changing historical worlds.

Its themes are vast and often internally contested: justice and sacrifice, sin and forgiveness, chosenness and universality, political rule and prophetic critique. Part of the Bible's power comes from exactly that range. It is read devotionally, legally, liturgically, politically, and literarily, which is why arguments over the text rarely remain confined to theology alone.
A foundational scriptural collection in Judaism and Christianity, translated and contested across centuries.

cover.alt: The Bible cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A foundational scriptural collection in Judaism and Christianity, translated and contested across centuries.
banningParagraphs: The Bible entered censorship debates as a religious text associated with scripture, translation, and religious authority. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around heresy and doctrinal control.

The earliest event currently captured here is Early modern period in Papal States, where Catholic censorship authorities restricted vernacular circulation. Authorities targeted unauthorized translations and editions seen as doctrinally dangerous. For scriptures, bans often attached to versions, translations, and modes of circulation rather than a blanket destruction of the text.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
